video_title: Bloodraven: what's the three-eyed raven's secret plan?
theory_name: Bloodraven's grand plan (Azor Ahai bloodline + Bran/Jon)
claim: Bloodraven (Brynden Rivers) has spent a century secretly guiding events -- and possibly the Targaryen bloodline itself -- toward producing and empowering Azor Ahai (culminating in Jon Snow) and a greenseer successor (Bran Stark), in order to save the world from the Long Night.
sub_claims: Brynden was drawn to the old gods and developed nascent greenseer/skinchanging/green-dream powers from an early age, before he disappeared beyond the Wall
Brynden merged with a weirwood beyond the Wall, becoming the three-eyed crow, and waited fifty years for Bran Stark
Brynden actively recruits and empowers Bran as a greenseer to fight the white walkers
Brynden secretly controls Lord Commander Mormont's raven to spy on and influence Jon Snow, helping him become Lord Commander and hinting he wants Jon to be King

Brynden and his Targaryen family have a generations-long fixation on the Azor Ahai prophecy, suggesting Brynden orchestrated a Targaryen breeding program culminating in Jon Snow as the prophesied hero
Brynden may be responsible for numerous other magical or coincidental events throughout the series
asx_verdict: Presented as the video's central and fairly confident reading (strongly argued via the raven/Jon and weirwood-merge evidence), though the 'century-long breeding program' extension is explicitly flagged as speculative ('there's no knowing how deep this goes').
notes: Umbrella video adjudicating one overarching theory (Bloodraven's grand plan) plus several distinct named sub-theories nested within it (Maynard Plumm=Bloodraven, Quaithe=Shiera Seastar, Bloodraven trained Euron Greyjoy) and a competing counter-theory (Bloodraven is secretly a villain) -- each emitted as its own theory line per spec. Heavy Dunk & Egg / TWOIAF backstory for Bloodraven's early life; heavy use of show material (Season 6 tower-of-joy reveal, Bran's arc) alongside book evidence.

paraphrase: Brynden's father was King Aegon IV Targaryen (blood of Valyria/dragon-fire magic) and his mother was a Blackwood, a First Men family whose heraldry is a weirwood tree (blood of the old gods) -- giving Brynden the blood of both Valyria and the First Men.
Brynden's longbow at the Battle of the Redgrass Field was made of weirwood, hinting he was drawn to the old gods from early on.
Brynden's pale skin and red eyes look just like a weirwood's face, with white bark and red sap eyes.
Rumors said Brynden could transform into a dog, control wolves, and spy with crows -- which sounds like skinchanging powers, similar to how Bran wargs into animals.
Brynden might also have had green dreams -- visions of the future, like Jojen Reed has -- and at the Night's Watch he might have seen the threat of the white walkers.

Brynden was drawn north beyond the Wall to a cave beneath a great weirwood tree, where he merged with the weirwood -- its roots growing through his body, extending his life and heightening his power as a greenseer (skinchanging into animals, seeing through trees' eyes, visions of past and future).
book_hint: beyond the Wall, the cave of the three-eyed crow (ADWD, Bran chapters)

paraphrase: Brynden sat dreaming, watching, and scheming for fifty years in the cave, waiting for Bran Stark.

Bran apparently has a special destiny as a greenseer like Brynden; Brynden visits Bran in dreams in the form of a three-eyed crow, tells him to fly and wake his magic, and sends Jojen to take Bran north.
Brynden says Bran is important for the war against the white walkers.

In the show, Bran uses his powers to spy on the white walkers with ravens and has psychic contact with the Night King, suggesting Brynden succeeds in empowering him to fight for life against the Long Night.
Lord Commander Mormont's raven talks -- usually just repeating words it hears, but sometimes it seems to understand what's happening and influences the story.
When the Night's Watch elects a new Lord Commander, the raven says 'Snow, snow, snow' and lands on Jon's shoulder, helping get Jon chosen as leader.
book_hint: ASOS, Jon chapters (election of Lord Commander)

paraphrase: The bird often says 'King' around Jon; in Book 5 it says 'King ... Snow, Jon Snow.'
Just before that, the raven appears in a dream Jon has where he fights 'dead men' with a burning sword -- Azor Ahai / Lightbringer imagery.
Many readers believe this raven is controlled from afar by Bloodraven, since he's known to use birds to spy and enter dreams -- probably using the bird to spy on the Night's Watch and influence Jon.
It looks like Brynden not only helps Jon lead the Watch but wants Jon to be King of Westeros.

In Season 6, Brynden shows Bran Jon's birth as the son of Lyanna Stark and Rhaegar Targaryen, making Jon 'the heir to the Iron Throne'; Jon has the blood of both the First Men and Valyria, just like Bloodraven.
spoken_quote: the heir to the Iron Throne

paraphrase: Brynden says 'There have always been Targaryens who dreamed of things to come' -- Targaryens who had visions of the future.
spoken_quote: There have always been Targaryens who dreamed of things to come

paraphrase: Rhaegar was into the Azor Ahai prophecy -- which might be why he ran off with Lyanna and fathered Jon.
Rhaegar's parents Aerys and Rhaella got married because a witch said Azor Ahai 'would be born of their line.'
spoken_quote: would be born of their line

paraphrase: Maester Aemon was into the Azor Ahai prophecy, and Brynden served with Aemon on the Wall for nineteen years.
King Aerys (II) was into prophecy, and Shiera Seastar was into 'ancient scrolls.'
Judging by the Aerys/Rhaella and Rhaegar/Lyanna pattern, it seems a particular Targaryen bloodline is expected to produce Azor Ahai.

Brynden's intense focus on stopping the Blackfyres may not have just been politics or his feud with Bittersteel -- he may have been protecting the bloodline he believed would produce the prophesied hero.
This bloodline-protection motive might explain why King Aerys (I) refused to have children, why Egg (Aegon V) was chosen as king, and even why Egg married a Blackwood with First Men blood.
The theory frames this as Bloodraven guiding the Targaryens on a hundred-year eugenic quest to breed Azor Ahai.

Brynden's godlike influence (entering dreams, seeing through trees, controlling animals) could extend to sending the Stark direwolf pups, the boar that killed King Robert, the bear that mated with Tormund, the weirwood dream that led Jaime to Brienne, Beric's magic in the weirwood cave, the Ghost of High Heart, Coldhands and Sam, and the Horn of Joramun.

video_title: Bloodraven: what's the three-eyed raven's secret plan?
claim: Maynard Plumm, the mysterious well-informed stranger in the Dunk & Egg story 'The Mystery Knight,' is secretly Bloodraven in disguise, using a magical glamour.
notes: Nested sub-theory within the Bloodraven's-grand-plan video; sourced entirely from the Dunk & Egg novella 'The Mystery Knight.'

paraphrase: In 'The Mystery Knight,' Daemon Blackfyre's son attempts a second Blackfyre rebellion, but Bloodraven's spies uncover the plot with the help of a suspicious informant, Maynard Plumm.
Plumm knows too much, makes weird references to Bloodraven, and there's something strange about his face.
Plumm disappears from the story just before Bloodraven arrives.
Plumm wears a 'moonstone brooch,' which suggests Bloodraven uses a glamour -- the same kind of magic Melisandre uses with her ruby.

video_title: Bloodraven: what's the three-eyed raven's secret plan?
claim: Quaithe, the masked woman who visits Daenerys in dreams with cryptic advice, is secretly Shiera Seastar -- Bloodraven's century-old lover -- continuing to work magic in the east on his behalf.
asx_verdict: The video explicitly says there's no strong evidence for this identification, but calls it a fun possibility.
notes: Nested sub-theory; the video itself flags it as weakly supported speculation, unlike the Plumm identification.

paraphrase: Quaithe is a mysterious masked woman who visits Dany in dreams and gives her cryptic advice, first appearing in Book/Season 2.
Both Quaithe and Shiera are mysterious magical women associated with stars.
Shiera was born a hundred years ago, so like Brynden she'd need magic to stay alive; both Shiera and her mother are said to use magic to preserve their beauty.
The mask Quaithe wears might hide Shiera's famous beauty.
The video states outright there's no strong evidence for the Quaithe-is-Shiera identification, even though it would be 'cool' if true.

video_title: Bloodraven: what's the three-eyed raven's secret plan?
claim: Bloodraven once attempted to contact and train young Euron Greyjoy as a greenseer by entering his dreams, but something went wrong, and the psychic trauma set Euron on his path toward his 'Crow's Eye' apocalyptic magical quest.
asx_verdict: Presented as speculative ('maybe'/'might') -- an unconfirmed possible explanation for Euron's magical obsession, framed by the video as a cautionary parallel to Bloodraven's ends-justify-means approach.
notes: Nested sub-theory; also serves the video's closing thematic argument about the costs of Bloodraven's methods.

paraphrase: Euron in the books is a mystical, scary figure: his ship is crewed by mutes, its deck painted red to hide blood, and he owns a magic horn called Dragonbinder.
Euron captures and tortures priests, warlocks, and pregnant women, planning a mass blood sacrifice to transform himself into a godlike being.
Euron says: 'These are the last days, when the world shall be broken and remade. A new god shall be born from the graves and charnel pits.'
spoken_quote: when the world shall be broken and remade. A new god shall be born from the graves and charnel pits

paraphrase: Euron calls himself 'the Crow’s Eye,' and his banner is a single red eye crowned by crows -- which might refer to Brynden's single red eye as the three-eyed crow.

In Book 4, Euron says that as a boy he dreamed he could fly, that if he just tried, if he leapt from a tower, he might fly -- which is just what happens to Bran when Brynden visits his dream and tells him to try to fly.
Maybe Brynden saw magic potential in young Euron and made contact hoping to train him as a greenseer, but when he flung the boy at psychic ice spikes something went wrong and Euron went rogue.
This psychic trauma from Bloodraven may have inadvertently inspired Euron's obsession with collecting magic artifacts, warlocks, and priests, and his plan to bring about his own apotheosis.

video_title: Bloodraven: what's the three-eyed raven's secret plan?
claim: Bloodraven is not a benevolent mentor but a secret villain: theories include that he wants to bodysnatch Bran's young body because his own is dying, that he is secretly helping the white walkers/Great Other destroy humanity, or that he is a puppet controlled by the children of the forest's psychic weirwood hivemind.
sub_claims: Bloodraven wants Bran's body because his own is dying (magical bodysnatching)
Bloodraven is secretly helping the white walkers/Great Other wipe out humanity
Bloodraven is manipulated/controlled by the children of the forest's psychic weirwood hivemind
asx_verdict: The video ultimately rejects a straightforward-villain reading, concluding Brynden is 'something more interesting than a bad guy' -- a man who does bad things for good reasons, at any cost -- and uses this to mount a thematic critique of ends-justify-means thinking.
notes: Counter-theory to the main 'Bloodraven's grand plan' theory; the video weighs villain evidence but argues for a morally grey 'gets good outcomes at any cost' reading instead, and closes on unintended-consequences beats shared with the Euron sub-theory.

paraphrase: Brynden killed his own half-brother Daemon Blackfyre and two of Daemon's sons at the Battle of the Redgrass Field to defeat the first Blackfyre Rebellion -- a kinslaying, one of the worst crimes in Westeros, committed for what he saw as the greater good.
book_hint: Battle of the Redgrass Field / TWOIAF

paraphrase: As Hand of the King, Brynden basically ruled the realm as a spymaster with a huge informer network -- it was said he had 'a thousand eyes and one' -- and he was so focused on hunting Blackfyre sympathizers that he ignored an ironborn invasion while the realm suffered drought.
At the Great Council to choose the next king, Bloodraven promised a surviving Blackfyre claimant he would not be killed if he came, then had him killed the moment he arrived.
Brynden argued that he 'sacrificed his own ... honor for the good of the realm,' but was still arrested by the newly-crowned King Egg and sent to the Wall.
spoken_quote: honor for the good of the realm

paraphrase: Bloodraven looks like a 'corpse,' a ghastly statue 'of twisted wood, old bone, and rotted wool,' living in a dark cave littered with skulls, and keeps company with a corpse-like man and creepy forest elves.
spoken_quote: of twisted wood, old bone, and rotted wool

paraphrase: Brynden tells Bran 'Never fear the darkness.'
Brynden tells Bran 'Darkness will make you strong.'
Maybe Brynden only wants Bran because his old body is dying and he plans to steal Bran's young body -- magical bodysnatching is established as a real thing in Book 5.
Others think Brynden is secretly helping the white walkers wipe out humanity.
Melisandre has a vision of Brynden in which she believes he is with the evil Great Other.
There is a possibility Brynden is manipulated by the children of the forest and their psychic weirwood hivemind.
Author George R.R. Martin has written other stories about evil, seductive alien hiveminds, offered as circumstantial support for a sinister weirwood-hivemind reading.
The video concludes that what we've seen in the text suggests Brynden is something more interesting than a straightforward bad guy -- a character who does bad things for good reasons.

He kills his half-brother to end a war, lies and betrays to keep the peace, and spies, assassinates, and manipulates all for the sake of the realm -- so he's not straight-up evil, more a man who gets good outcomes at any cost.

Brynden lures a crippled eight-year-old Bran on a dangerous journey that gets his friends killed, changing Bran into something he never wanted to become; Bran had hoped Brynden would 'fix his legs' but never signed up to become a tree boy, and by the time he arrives he hardly has a choice.
Brynden manipulates Bran, at one point magically editing Bran's memories so that he forgets Jaime Lannister crippling him.
Brynden's agent Coldhands secretly feeds Bran human flesh, and possibly the dead body of Jojen Reed.
In the show, a side-effect of Bran's power is that he becomes robotic and dehumanized -- Meera says it's like Bran died.

As a thematic counterpoint, when Stannis considers killing a child to win the realm, he asks 'what is the life of one ... boy against a kingdom?' and Davos replies 'Everything' -- suggesting sacrificing basic humanity and compassion undermines the very thing being saved.
spoken_quote: what is the life of one

paraphrase: Davos's one-word reply to Stannis is simply 'Everything.'

In Bran's Book 1 dream, Brynden tells Bran 'Fly or die' to force him to wake his magic; Bran flies over spikes of ice but sees 'the bones of a thousand other dreamers impaled' below, hinting Brynden has tried this method on other students during his fifty years in the cave.
The 'bones of a thousand other dreamers impaled' seen below in the dream is offered as further evidence of past failed students.
spoken_quote: the bones of a thousand other dreamers impaled

paraphrase: Brynden's harsh political control of Westeros as Hand of the King may have just fed more discontent and rebellion, suggesting his ends-justify-means approach can breed more darkness rather than less.
